title: Talking about removing subcutaneous fatty lipomas

transcript.text: Hello, my name is Kyle Pounds, and this video is talking about subcutaneous fatty lipoma removal,
Because that's what I did to myself about one month and four days ago.
So I'll talk about the process from the beginning to the end.
Back in high school, I didn't have any lipomas.
There's nothing.
You know, 10 years ago, I don't remember having anything.
And then I kind of noticed a lump on my arm.
You know, and it was weird how it come, because it's like, all of a sudden, I noticed this big lump on my arm.
So I think when it was really small, I just didn't think about it until it got really big.
I mean, it took years to do, and all of a sudden, it's like, oh my God, I got this lipoma.
And then it just continued to get larger and larger and larger.
And then I got this one in my leg, which was even bigger, and it was even bigger than the one in my alarm.
Originally, my mom wanted me to get him removed, but I didn't want to, because I didn't have insurance and I didn't have any money.
But then my mom kept telling me she wanted me to remove him.
And then I started feeling him, and then it got in my head that I had to get him removed,
Because I was just feeling him all the time on my leg.
And I could feel them, even when I put my hand on the desk, I could feel the extra pressure in my arm.
And I felt like it was even making my two fingers numb, although that's probably not the main reason.
The main reason was just, if you put your elbows on the table for too long, you get the carpal tunnel.
But it wasn't that.
But anyways, it does just, I could feel it on me, and then people asking me what they are,

And it says here that it affects one out of every thousand people,
And mostly women get them, and it affects people in their 40s, 50s, and 60s.

I know I'm going to continue to grow them throughout my life and have to get them out.
But since I did it myself, it's not daunting, and I actually enjoy it.
So the process of me doing it is I did research.

She got the number from the University of Seattle Medical Center,
And I called them a few times, but they found out, or I told them,
That I'm not an actual official Washington citizen,

To get them removed there for all of them.

Insurance does not cover these because they're a cosmetic deformity.
They're not cancerous.
They're benign.

Frozen hot dogs.
